g.data-narration: Welcome to Line Segment Clipping - a fundamental computer graphics operation for rendering visible portions of lines.
Line clipping determines which portions of a line are visible within a clip window, essential for efficient graphics rendering.
g.text: • Prevents unnecessary computation for off-screen graphics
• Ensures only visible portions are rendered on display devices
• Critical for 3D graphics pipelines and view transformations

g.data-narration: The clip window is a rectangular region in world coordinates; the viewport displays clipped content on the output screen.
g.text: Defined by: Xmin, Xmax, Ymin, Ymax
Any line outside must be clipped

g.data-narration: The Cohen-Sutherland algorithm uses 4-bit region codes to classify endpoints relative to the clip window boundaries.
g.text: Each endpoint gets a 4-bit code representing its position relative to window:

g.data-narration: Visual representation of how each region is coded - the window is divided into 9 regions with unique bit patterns.
The algorithm first checks trivial cases: accept if both endpoints inside (0000), reject if completely outside.
g.text: • Both endpoints share a set bit

g.data-narration: The algorithm iteratively clips endpoints against window boundaries until accepted, rejected, or fully clipped.
Intersection points are calculated using parametric equations: t equals the normalized distance to each boundary.
g.text: For clipping against vertical boundaries (x = Xmin or Xmax):
For clipping against horizontal boundaries (y = Ymin or Ymax):

g.data-narration: Walk through clipping P1(100,50) to P2(200,150) against window Xmin=120, Xmax=180, Ymin=80, Ymax=140.
The Liang-Barsky algorithm uses parametric equations for more efficient line clipping with O(1) complexity.
g.text: Uses parametric equation: P(t) = P1 + t(P2 - P1), where 0 ≤ t ≤ 1
More efficient than Cohen-Sutherland - computes entering(t1) and leaving(t2) parameters
Single pass algorithm with O(1) complexity

g.data-narration: Four inequalities define the window boundaries in parametric form - p values are negated deltas, q values are differences.
For each boundary, compute r = q/p, then update t1 (entering) as max and t2 (leaving) as min values.
g.text: Update Rules for t1 and t2
For each boundary (p ≠ 0): compute r = q/p, then:
if p < 0: t1 = max(t1, r) // entering
if p > 0: t2 = min(t2, r) // leaving
If p = 0 and q < 0: reject (parallel and outside)

g.data-narration: Example: Clip line P1(50,20) to P2(150,100) with window X[80,120], Y[40,80]. Let's compute step by step.
Midpoint subdivision repeatedly bisects partially visible lines, suitable for parallel processing and hardware implementation.
g.text: Divide-and-conquer approach - repeatedly bisects line segments:
1. Compute midpoint of segment | 2. Determine region code of midpoint
3. Keep half containing inside endpoint | 4. Repeat until small enough or trivially determined

g.data-narration: Compare the three algorithms: Cohen-Sutherland is most common, Liang-Barsky is most efficient, Midpoint suits parallel systems.
Remember: region codes follow Top-Bottom-Right-Left order, check trivial cases first, and practice numerical problems.
g.text: Memorize bit positions: Top(8), Bottom(4), Right(2), Left(1) | Always check trivial accept/reject first | Practice intersection calculations
